retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Stricture -- Etiology -- Malignant Stricture
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal adenocarcinoma Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma Metastatic esophageal neoplasm - usually from lung cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Stricture -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant stricture develops from intrinsic direct proliferation and invasion of cancer cells from the luminal mucosa. Adenocarcinoma commonly arises from the lower part of the esophagus, and squamous cell carcinoma frequently occurs in the middle and upper parts of the esophagus. Very rarely, the direct growth of lung tumor mass or mediastinal lymph node enlargement can also produce stricture.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars is FDA approved for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), plaque psoriasis (PsO), inflammatory bowel disease (IBD): adult Crohn disease (CD), ulcerative colitis (UC), granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) (Wegener granulomatosis), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM), non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer, metastatic colorectal cancer, metastatic renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) associated breast cancer, and HER2 associated gastric (metastatic) or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). Biosimilars are biotherapeutic agents similar to their respective original biologic product (reference product). This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in managing patients with inflammatory diseases and the use of biosimilars.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, the pathophysiologic mechanism of esophageal adenocarcinoma is repeated exposure of the lower esophagus to stomach acid secondary to chronic GERD, which leads to the replacement of the normal squamous epithelium with metaplastic columnar epithelium (ie, Barrett esophagus). The metaplastic epithelium may progressively undergo dysplastic changes, classified as low-grade or high-grade dysplasia, based on the degree of cellular abnormalities. High-grade dysplasia can progress to invasive adenocarcinoma, where cancer cells invade the underlying layers of the esophageal wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenocarcinoma(Archived) -- Etiology -- Adenocarcinoma of the Pancreas
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental risk factors contributing to pancreatic adenocarcinoma include smoking, high fasting plasma glucose, high body mass index, western diet, and alcohol consumption, prolonged use of aspirin. [11] Hereditary risk factors also contribute to the risk, with up to 10 percent of individuals with pancreatic cancer having a family history. [12] [13] ABO blood type has been associated with several gastrointestinal cancers, including pancreatic cancer as well as cystic fibrosis, with 2 to 5-fold higher risk in patients who have undergone a lung transplant. [14] [15] [16] [17] [18] The amount of cigarette smoking significantly increases the risk of adenocarcinoma of the pancreas, [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] a population-attributable fraction of deaths due to pancreatic cancer associated with smoking is 11% to 32%. [24] Other important risk factors include H. pylori in gastric adenocarcinoma [25] , gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and alcohol in esophageal adenocarcinoma. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: For esophageal SCC, the squamous epithelium undergoes dysplastic changes, including nuclear enlargement, hyperchromasia, and loss of polarity. Dysplasia is categorized as either low-grade or high-grade, depending on the degree of these abnormal changes. The dysplastic changes affect the entire thickness of the epithelium, but the basement membrane remains intact. As the tumor progresses, the cancer cells breach the basement membrane and invade the underlying layers of the esophageal wall. The tumor cells are organized in sheets, nests, or individual cells, exhibiting varying degrees of keratinization. Esophageal SCC is graded based on the level of keratinization, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. Well-differentiated tumors display abundant keratinization and low mitotic activity, while poorly differentiated tumors show minimal keratinization and high mitotic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Epithelial Cell -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another clinical concern that relates to epithelial cells is metaplasia. Metaplasia is when one type of cell converts to another due to environmental stressors or changes. This process can occur in physiological or pathological conditions. For instance, a study on the cervical epithelium from postpartum women has shown the replacement of columnar epithelial tissue with squamous epithelial cells in the physiologic healing process of the postpartum period. [22] Pathologic metaplasia is more likely to be dysplastic, which can become malignant. One relatively common example of pathologic metaplasia is Barrett's esophagus. The esophagus is typically lined by squamous epithelium. When patients have uncontrolled gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), the constant stress induced by acid from the stomach causes the squamous cells of the esophagus to become mucin-producing columnar cells. The mucin-producing columnar cells are better equipped to handle the stress of the stomach acid, preventing erosion of the esophagus. If the GERD receives proper treatment, the columnar cells may revert to squamous cells; however, if the stressor goes untreated, metaplasia may progress to dysplasia, which can become malignant. Several studies have demonstrated that Barrett's esophagus increases predisposition to esophageal adenocarcinoma. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Pitfalls to Avoid in Esophageal Cancer Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pitfalls in esophageal cancer management include delayed or incorrect diagnosis, particularly in patients with atypical symptoms or those empirically treated for GERD without further evaluation. Inadequate staging, which fails to detect distant metastases or accurately assess lymph node involvement, can lead to inappropriate treatment decisions. Additionally, insufficient attention to nutritional support throughout the cancer journey can result in significant morbidity and impaired quality of life. Effective care coordination among the multidisciplinary team is essential to avoid fragmented, suboptimal care and poor patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Barrett Esophagus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with forceps biopsies is the gold-standard diagnostic test. Wide-area transepithelial sampling with brushing of the entire surface of the Barrett epithelium may add to the yield of standard endoscopy with forceps biopsies; this technique has not yet been incorporated into the ACG guideline. High-definition white light and chromoendoscopy, using topical acetic acid or electronic chromoendoscopy, increase sensitivity and specificity for identifying dysplasia and adenocarcinoma. [17] Artificial intelligence is being evaluated in the United States and the Netherlands to develop a system that will locate neoplasia and the most abnormal areas as the optimal sites for biopsy. [18] However, the cost of sedated endoscopy and the small risk of complications have raised interest in developing alternative diagnostic testing. Such alternatives include unsedated transnasal endoscopy that nonclinicians can perform and using one of several commercially available swallowable capsule sponges combined with a biomarker such as a methylated deoxynucleic acid marker or trefoil factor 3.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal cancer is classified into 2 main types based on histologic features: squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma. In recent decades, the incidence of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma has decreased. In contrast, esophageal adenocarcinoma has risen significantly in the United States, particularly in the distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, mainly due to the prevalence of Barrett's esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinoembryonic Antigen -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a nonspecific serum biomarker that is elevated in many malignancies, including colorectal cancer, medullary thyroid cancer, breast cancer, and mucinous ovarian cancer. CEA was first detected in colon cancer cells by Freedman and Gold and was eventually found in various other epithelial cells in the stomach , tongue, esophagus, cervix, and prostate. [1] This glycoprotein has a molecular weight of 200 kDa and is normally derived from fetal embryonic endodermal epithelium, controlled by fetal oncogenes.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Esophagus -- Clinical Significance -- Esophageal carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal carcinomas are usually either squamous cell carcinomas or adenocarcinomas. As esophageal cancer expands, the lumen of the esophagus narrows down, and dysphagia occurs due to mechanical obstruction. Upper endoscopy helps with, and biopsy confirms the diagnosis of esophageal cancer. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Glandular Cells (AGS) -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: HPV infection is a significant risk factor for developing endocervical adenocarcinomas. [7] HPV is a DNA virus capable of penetrating through the basal cell layer of the cervical mucosa. HPV surface proteins bind to integrins, a membrane receptor in the epithelial cells. A secondary receptor might be involved in stabilizing the HPV in the cell and integration with host DNA. The HPV DNA replicates in the host cells and reaches the surface as the basal cells mature. Host cell factors are required for HPV replication and proliferation. The interaction of host cell factors with the LCR region of the HPV DNA leads to transcription of E6 and E7 proteins: E6 and E7 inactivate tumor suppressor genes and cyclin-dependent kinases. HPV E6 binds to P53 and leads to its increased ubiquitination while E7 binds to the Rb tumor suppressor gene, the final outcome of these interactions in increased DNA synthesis and cell proliferation. More damaged DNA increases over time, leading to cumulative accumulation of mutations and finally cervical dysplasia and malignancy. [3] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Amylase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Cases of amylase-producing multiple myeloma have been described. Increased amylase activity in most patients results from salivary-type hyperamylasemia—sialyl salivary type. [59] A common feature of the myeloma cell lines associated with hyperamylasemia is the presence of a chromosome 1 translocation, which harbors the gene for amylase. The link does not seem to be specific to any particular immunoglobulin class. The onset of hyperamylasemia is reported to be associated with rapid disease progression, extensive bone destruction, and increased mortality. Consequently, serum amylase activity may serve as a valuable prognostic "tumor marker" in patients with multiple myeloma, with activity decreasing in response to treatment and increasing during periods of relapse. [60] [61] The amylase isoenzyme in cases of ruptured ectopic pregnancy is not well characterized. In severe cases that are diagnosed late, the elevated isoenzyme may be P-AMY due to pancreatic involvement associated with peritonitis, despite the presence of S-AMY in the fallopian tube. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Barrett Metaplasia (Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: There are not any specific complications of Barrett's esophagus per se. The clinicians fear its progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma as the main complication and aim to prevent this development.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Histopathology -- Malignancy-Related Ulcers
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 histologic types accounting for most esophageal cancers are adenocarcinoma and SCC. Primary esophageal lymphoma and sarcoma are extremely rare but have been reported in the literature, accounting for less than 1% of primary esophageal tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as a complication of the esophageal disorder اضطراب المريء.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2921
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified as اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified under the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” and are clinically associated with “تعاطي الكحول” (alcohol consumption), indicating that alcohol use is a relevant factor in the development or exacerbation of these esophageal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: The medical classification framework designates “esophageal disorders” as belonging to the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” (other esophageal disorders), indicating that these conditions are grouped under a broader label of miscellaneous esophageal pathology. In addition, the term “Without” is noted to be associated with esophageal disorders, reflecting that the absence of specific clinical features or findings is a relevant descriptor when evaluating or documenting these gastrointestinal conditions. This relationship underscores the importance of both categorical classification and descriptive terminology in the assessment and management of esophageal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1135
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs—including the peritoneum—and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these neoplasms is a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic assessment and coordinated management across these organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus is linked to the procedural category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, indicating that it is considered an alternative or related mapping of that endoscopic examination. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is classified under the same category of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, reflecting its role as a diagnostic approach that includes tissue sampling. Together, these statements illustrate that both the therapeutic Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and the diagnostic Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy belong to the broader domain of upper gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures, highlighting their complementary roles in evaluating and managing esophageal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized within enzymology as an applicable descriptor for قَرْهَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), تضيُّق الصمام المترالي (mitral valve stenosis), and عضال غدي (glandular tumor). In enzymatic studies and diagnostic contexts, these three conditions can be evaluated using the permitted qualifier, indicating that specific enzymatic parameters or markers are relevant for assessing gastric ulcer pathology, the hemodynamic impact of mitral valve stenosis, and the biochemical characteristics of glandular tumors. This linkage underscores the role of enzymology in providing standardized qualifiers that facilitate consistent interpretation of laboratory and clinical findings across diverse disease entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 961
retrieved_docs.content: associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding), underscoring the risk of bleeding complications in patients with advanced hepatic disease. Overall, the knowledge base connects variceal conditions of the esophagus, broader esophageal disorders, venous and lymphatic disease classifications, alcohol‑related liver injury, and severe liver disease, illustrating how these entities interrelate in the context of diagnosis, classification, and clinical risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: of esophageal disorders and also as a member of دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, underscoring the interconnected nature of liver pathology with both esophageal and gastrointestinal disease processes. Finally, the clinical association between تعاطي الكحول and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة reinforces the broader impact of alcohol consumption on gastrointestinal health, linking lifestyle factors to a spectrum of vascular, esophageal, and hepatic conditions. This integrated network of classifications and clinical relationships emphasizes the importance of recognizing overlapping disease mechanisms and risk factors in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified under the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” (other esophageal disorders). Within this classification, the SMQ titled “الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد” (liver failure, hepatic fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other conditions associated with liver damage) is listed as a member of the esophageal disorders group. Although liver pathology is not a primary manifestation of esophageal disease, this coding framework groups these hepatic conditions as members of the broader esophageal disorder category, likely to capture related comorbidities or overlapping clinical presentations. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians and medical coders document and manage patients who present with both esophageal and liver abnormalities in an integrated manner.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 212
retrieved_docs.content: therapeutic actions comprise ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal stenting). Diagnostic imaging such as upper gastrointestinal X‑ray is performed directly at the esophageal site (direct_procedure_site_of). This integrated network of procedural classifications, morphological descriptors, and disease entities supports clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for esophageal, variceal, and related upper‑gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1516
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Diagnosis/Diseases Component” is classified as a sub‑element of both “اضطراب في عنق الرحم” (cervical disorder) and “اضْطِرابُ المريء” (esophageal disorder), indicating that this diagnostic component is relevant to the evaluation and categorization of diseases affecting the cervix as well as the esophagus. This dual parentage reflects its role in the clinical assessment of disorders in these distinct anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2882
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy (تنظير مريئي) is classified within the broader category of other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures, indicating that it is one of several techniques used to evaluate the gastrointestinal tract and is part of a wider set of diagnostic tools for gastrointestinal assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the bladder, cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs – including the peritoneum, and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these conditions represents a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic evaluation, appropriate staging, and coordinated treatment strategies across these cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures encompass a range of examinations used to evaluate the digestive tract, and within this broader group, التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي (gastrointestinal endoscopy) serves as a parent category for specific techniques such as تنظير مريئي (esophageal endoscopy). Thus, تنظير مريئي is classified as a subtype of التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي and is included among other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures, highlighting its role in the systematic assessment of esophageal pathology.
